sentences.text: The tithe ships came to Halcyon on the first day of the quarter, the way weather comes to a world that still has weather: on schedule, indifferent, and larger than anything you could do about it.
Vega stood on Dock Nine with her thumbs hooked in her harness and counted hulls the way her mother had taught her to count storms.
Four corvettes, grey as slag.
One collier with its belly doors already open.
And behind them, keeping a distance that was itself a kind of speech, the Synod tithe-barge *Obedient Sum*, so large it had a horizon.
"They brought the barge," Kestrel said.
Her cousin was eleven that year and small for it, and she had climbed the loading gantry to see, because no one had yet taught her that some things are worse when seen clearly.
"Vega.
They brought the *barge*."
"I can see it."
"Last quarter they only sent the corvettes."
"I can see it, Kes."
The dockmaster's klaxon sounded three times — the pattern that meant *stand to and be counted*, which everyone on Halcyon had learned the way you learn a hymn: without deciding to.
Around them the dock crews stopped pretending to work.
Ore carts stood where their handlers left them.
Somewhere down the row a welding arc went out and did not restart, and the dark it left behind felt louder than the klaxon.
Hale limped up the ramp with his cap off, which he only did for funerals.
"Assessor's aboard," he said.
"New one.
Young.
The old one, Brann — reassigned."
He said *reassigned* the way you'd say a man's name at his wake.
"What do they want this time?"
Vega asked.
"We paid the quarter.
We paid it twice, after the audit."
"Ore.
Alloys.
The number-four fabricator."
Hale's jaw worked.
"And crews."
The word sat on the dock like a dropped load.
*Crews.*
The tithe had always been things — you could hate a tithe of things cleanly, the way you hate rust.
Kaine's assessors weighed your ingots, stamped your manifests, and left you poorer and whole.
A tithe of people was a different arithmetic.
"They can't," Kestrel said, from the gantry.
"That's not — Hale, that's not the *rate*.
The rate is posted.
It's *posted*."

"Rates are what the strong publish about the weak," said a voice below them, and Vega turned and got her first look at Assessor Sirin of the Iron Synod, who would matter to her for the rest of her life, though neither of them knew it yet.
Sirin was younger than the uniform wanted her to be, with the Synod's iron-and-ash sash worn slightly wrong, as if her hands disagreed with it every morning.
Behind her a Synod quartermaster — TALLOW, said the stencil on his chest-plate — was already counting the dock crew with a lens that clicked.
Click.
Click.
Each click was a person.
"You're the new assessor," Hale said.

"I am the instrument of the assessment," Sirin said, and Vega heard it then, the thing she would spend years learning to name: the voice of someone reciting a sentence they had stopped believing partway through, and finishing it anyway because the sentence was load-bearing.
"The posted rate—" Kestrel began.
"The posted rate funds a fleet that no longer exists," Sirin said, not unkindly, which made it worse.
"The Hegemon requires the fleet that is replacing it.
Warlord Kaine requires what the Hegemon requires.
The mathematics descend."
She looked up at Kestrel on the gantry, a small girl above a grey harbor, and something in the assessor's face moved and was put away.
"Get your sister down from there before the quartermaster counts her."
"Cousin," said Vega.
"Get your cousin down from there before the quartermaster counts her."
Vega did not move.

On the manifest board behind Sirin, the collier's loading arms had begun to swing, and the first of Halcyon's ore — *their* ore, dug by hands she knew, owed against a winter she could already feel — went up the belly of the Synod ship in a river of grey.
The dock lights caught the dust of it.
It looked, she thought, like the station itself was being inhaled.
"I want to file a protest," she said.
Tallow's lens clicked at her.
"Noted," he said, in the tone of a man stamping a form that fed a shredder.
"With a person," Vega said.
"I want to file a protest with a person."
Sirin met her eyes, and for a held breath the recitation stopped.
"So do I," the assessor said, too quietly for the lens to hear.
Then the sash straightened itself, and the voice came back on duty.
"Ore first.
Alloys second.
The fabricator will be uninstalled by Synod engineers; interference is sabotage; sabotage is tithed at the rate of one crew per incident."
She turned.
She had a good turn, parade-ground, wasted on a dock full of miners.
"The crew levy is deferred," she added, to the manifest board, to nobody, "pending my assessment of the station's yield curve.
Quartermaster — stop counting."
The lens stopped clicking.

It was, Vega understood later, the first thing Sirin ever stole from the Iron Synod, and she stole it for strangers: one quarter, sixty names, deferred on the authority of a junior assessor's invented curve.
At the time it only felt like weather breaking — the barge unclenching, the corvettes taking their shadows elsewhere, the dock crews breathing out in a ragged, furious, useless relief.
Hale put his cap back on.
"That one won't last," he said, watching Sirin's launch fall away toward the *Obedient Sum*.
"Kaine eats the ones who defer."
"Then why did she do it?"
"Same reason you filed a protest with a shredder."
He took her shoulder, and his hand was heavier than it used to be.
"Because the arithmetic is wrong, and some people can't make themselves carry the remainder."
He looked at the grey river still pouring out of his station, and at Kestrel climbing down at last, jaw set, already older than the morning.
"Your mother could not.
You will not.
It's going to cost you what it cost her, Vega.
I'd stop you if I thought grief was worse than what's coming instead."
Above them the *Obedient Sum* swallowed the last of the quarter's ore and closed its doors, and the sound the seals made was soft, and final, and did not echo at all.
That night Vega wrote three names in the maintenance log's margin, where the Synod's auditors never looked because margins had no yield: *Brann, reassigned.
Sirin, deferred us.
Kaine.*
The first two so she would remember them.
The third so she would never need to.

Twelve years before the *Obedient Sum* ever came to Halcyon, the carrier *Sovereign Lash* hung at anchor over Ferrus, drinking alloys out of the smelter moons, and in her belly, on Creche Deck Four, two hundred children sat with their hands flat on the tables where the wardens could count them, waiting for the meal bell.
The Synod did not believe in telling children the time.
It believed in telling them what came next.
What came next arrived as bells — the work bell, the wash bell, the meal bell — and before every meal, in the pause where other childhoods keep grace, the Doctrine.
*One fleet.
One order.
One will.
The hand that flies alone falls alone.
I am not the hand.
I am the Will.*
Orion was eleven that year, Row Nine, Seat Fourteen, and he had lately begun committing the first crime of his life.
It was this: he moved his mouth and said nothing.
From inside that small silence the Doctrine sounded different — not like two hundred children speaking, but like the ship speaking through them, the way wind will use a gap in a hull for a throat.
He had not yet decided what was wrong with that.
The deciding would take him nine more years and a colony's worth of ash, but every defection begins somewhere, and his began as a boy mouthing words and listening to what said them.
On fourth-days he had a dispensation to visit gun deck six, because his father was Kel Orion, gunnery chief, thirty years aboard the *Lash* and as much a part of her as the keel.
Kel would set him on an ammunition crate, out of the crews' way, and let him watch the batteries drill: forty men moving like one animal with forty hands, and his father's voice moving them.
"A ship alone is a question nobody asked," Kel told him once, wiping recoil grease from his knuckles.
"You'll hear free-worlders talk about flying alone.
Free flight."
He said it the way you name a disease you have been vaccinated against.
"Free flight is chaos, boy.
A battery fires together or it's noise.
A fleet flies together or it's dying strangers.
There is no alone that isn't falling."

Orion, who loved his father with the whole undiscussed weight of a creche child's fourth-days, believed him for years, and this was the thing he could never make the free-born understand afterward: none of it was stupid.
None of it was mad.
It was all true the way a gun is true.
It simply was not true of people, and the Synod had decided never to check.
Sable came aboard with the spring musters, off a transfer tender, eight years old, carrying a duffel bigger than her shadow.
She was kin — Kel's brother's daughter — which on a Synod carrier meant precisely nothing and secretly everything, because the Synod did not post families together.
Kin in one compartment is a faction.
Her chit read: hull-orphan; parents reassigned to separate ships whose names were operational information, and therefore not hers to have.
The creche warden gave her to Orion as line-elder, the way you'd hand someone a lamp.
She recited the Doctrine perfectly.
She recited everything perfectly.
She believed it the way the deck believed in gravity — not as an opinion, as a floor.
"You said it wrong," she told him, his first week as her line-elder, under the amber night-cycle lights.
He went very still.
Reporting was a virtue; the creche was built of small informers.
"You didn't say it at all," Sable said.
"I watched your mouth.
It just walks along under the words."
She studied him with her head tipped, a small auditor.
"Say it with me and I won't have seen it."

So every meal after that he said it aloud with her, and she was satisfied, and that was the whole of the bargain that bound them: she kept his silence and he kept her company, kin keeping kin in a place engineered against the noun.

In the autumn, Warlord Kaine came aboard the *Sovereign Lash*, and the ship mustered on the hangar deck — crews, pilots, and the creche rows down front, because the Synod believed instruction should start early.
Orion stood at parade rest between Sable and the cold, and watched a captain die.
His name was Deverr.

He commanded an escort squadron off a shielded moon that had refused its assessment, and he had been ordered to spend that squadron proving the moon's shield had a price, and instead he had looked at the mathematics, and broken off, and brought every one of his forty pilots home.
The charge was read by a young lieutenant named Moros — a neat man with careful corners, who folded the charge sheet afterward like a napkin at a formal table.
Orion never again in his life watched a man fold paper precisely without tasting bile.
"Captain Deverr has stolen from you," Kaine said to the assembled ship.
He had a low voice; it made you leant toward him, which he knew.
"Not ships.
Ships are iron; we pour more.
He has stolen the certainty of the man on your wing.
He was ordered to spend, and he chose to keep.
A fleet where captains choose is not a fleet.
It is an argument wearing hulls.
The tithe of a captain who retreats is one captain."
He did not raise the pistol so much as let it arrive.
"The mathematics descend," he said, and fired.

Deverr's forty pilots stood in ranks and watched him fall, alive because of him, at attention because of Kaine, and that was the arithmetic that would not leave the boy in Row Nine alone: the captain had kept the men and lost the Will; the Will had kept nothing, and called the keeping theft.
Orion stood at parade rest with his mouth walking under the Doctrine, and inside the silence something finished changing shape.
"He fell alone," Sable whispered beside him, awed, believing.
"You heard it.
He flew alone from the order."
"He flew everyone home," Orion said.
"Home isn't a heading," said the eight-year-old the Synod had built, and the terrible thing, the thing that kept him loving her anyway, was that she meant it gently.
She was trying to console him with the floor.
Afterward Kaine paused at the rail of the dais, looked down at the lieutenant folding the charge sheet, and said, "Your name."
"Moros, Warlord."
"You read well," Kaine said, in the tone of a man marking a page to return to, and moved on with his staff.
Moros kept the folded charge like a decoration.
Orion, eleven, hating him with a child's clean totality, did not yet know that the neat man would one day be the Synod's executioner, or that the hatred would outlive the uniform they both wore.
Sable watched Moros too — but what her face held was closer to study, an apprentice's look, and that also would matter later, and also could not be known that day by anyone but this book.
That night Orion shook in his bunk, quietly, the way you learned to do anything in the creche, and it was Sable who padded down the row and found him.
She did not ask what was wrong.
Where they lived, the question was a formality; everything was wrong, on schedule.
She pressed something warm into his hand: a prayer coin, thumb-worn, the Will's sigil stamped on one face and the other face blank.
"My mother put it in my hand on the transfer gantry," she said.
"She said the stamped side is the fleet's.
The empty side is yours.
It's for the hand that falls."
She closed his fingers over it with both of hers, a small formal ceremony of her own invention, kin to kin.
"You looked like you were falling," she said, and went back to her bunk to sleep the sleep of the entirely convinced.

Orion put the coin in his boot, where inspection did not go, and lay awake while the engines said the Doctrine all night in their one voice — *one will, one will, one will* — and the question that would someday cost him his name, his father, and his ship formed itself in the dark, simple as a hull gap, patient as wind: *Whose?*
The assessor's invented curve bought Halcyon two quarters.
In the third quarter the fleet found an error in her arithmetic, and corrected it.
The *Obedient Sum* came back with the correction.
Sixty names, the same sixty the lens had counted and the young assessor had deferred, read off a corrected manifest by a quartermaster who did not look up.
Kestrel's mother was the fourth name.
Her father was the fortieth.
He was a rigger; riggers rate yard-grade; that was the whole of the explanation the manifest offered, and manifests do not take protests, with a person or otherwise.
The deferral, it turned out, had not saved the counting.
It had only saved it for later.
Kestrel was twelve that year, and lived on the cold spur now, in the dorm Halcyon kept for the children the levy left behind.
Vega came when her shifts let her, which was rarely, because Vega had taken her aunt's dock tokens and her uncle's rigging tokens and her own, and was working all three.
She would arrive smelling of ozone and flux, sit on the end of Kestrel's bunk, fall asleep mid-sentence sitting up, and be gone before the work bell.
They were a family of margins now.
Kestrel understood the arithmetic of it and hated it politely, the way you hate weather from indoors.
The dorm's other economy was run by Rook.
Rook was thirteen, off a Ferrus collier, orphaned by an earlier quarter of the same mathematics, and he played cards the way other people kept accounts.
Everyone knew he cheated.
It took Kestrel a month of watching to learn the actual scandal: he cheated in both directions.
When the little ones were losing their sweet-ration too fast, cards migrated home to them through his sleeves, and the game stayed alive, and nobody starved out of it.
"A game everybody loses is over by supper," he told her, dealing.
"You want it to last, somebody has to cheat fair."
He taught her Quarters, and marked her progress, and issued exactly one rule, the dorm's whole constitution: *never bet what you can't laugh off.*
She learned the rule.
Then she went up to the loft and broke it.
The loft was her father's — a rigger's radio shack over the Dock Nine gantries, built out of manifest errors, the parts that fall off inventories when inventories are counted by tired men.
The Synod had sealed the transmitter mast on tithe day; it had not occurred to anyone to seal a child.
On the first eighth-night after the *Sum* sailed, Kestrel warmed the set the way she'd watched her father do it, tuned to the open band, aimed the whip at the outbound corridor, and pressed the key.
"This is Kestrel, on Halcyon, for the crews of the third-quarter levy."
Her voice came back at her off the loft's tin wall, smaller than she'd planned.
"Sixty names.
I'm going to read them.
Somebody write them down."
She read all sixty.
It took eleven minutes.
Nothing answered but the carrier hiss, which is the sound the lane makes when it is politely declining to care.
She did it again the next eighth-night, and the next, and the fourth, dry-mouthed, to nobody.
On the fifth eighth-night, at the thirty-first name, the static organized itself into a voice.
"Halcyon broadcaster."
A girl's voice, young, level, wearing procedure like a pressed uniform.
"You are transmitting on a monitored band.
Cease transmission."
Kestrel's heart went up the mast.
She keyed back: "Make me."
A pause, five seconds of hiss, long enough for a signals rating on a barge four light-minutes gone to make the first undocumented decision of her life.
Then: "Say the names again.
From the beginning.
Slower."
That was how it started.
Not with mercy — with a correction.

The voice belonged to Signal Watch Three, night watch, aboard the tithe-barge *Obedient Sum*, and Signal Watch Three believed — flatly, entirely, the way you believe in deck-gravity — that the tithe was not theft.
The fleet fed the worlds that fed it; the mathematics descended; the levy was lawful.
But a manifest, said the voice, should be *correct*, and a child reading sixty names into an open band implied somewhere a manifest was being doubted, and that could not stand.
She copied all sixty.
She read them back without one error.
Numbers, Kestrel would think later, were the first thing they trusted each other with, because numbers were the one thing both their worlds agreed were sacred.
After that, every eighth-night, the band was open.
They traded true things, one for one, that being the tariff two suspicious children invent independently.

Kestrel described rain — the voice had been born in a creche between decks and asked, seriously, *what does weather do* — and got back the inside of the *Sum*: corridors of numbered cages for ore, the smell of counted air, a hymn the conscripts sang that was mostly multiplication.
Kestrel gave the bread-stall smell of the dock ring before the tithe.

The voice gave the exact tonnage the *Sum* had swallowed at Halcyon, which she was not supposed to have and recited anyway, because it was *correct*, and correctness was the only rebellion she had a word for yet.
It did not stay gentle.
Trades between enemies never do.
"You took my mother," Kestrel said, one eighth-night, because it had been sitting on the key for weeks.
"The levy took your mother.
I am a signals rating."
"You're the voice of the thing that took her."
The hiss ran long.
Kestrel counted it: nine seconds, the length of a decision.
"Yes," the voice said.
"And you are the voice of the place that feeds it, so we are both employed by arithmetic.
Do you want the manifest checked or not?"
"Checked," Kestrel said, hating her, needing her, twelve years old with her thumb white on the key.

It took three more quarters, and it was never explained what it cost, and some nights Signal Watch Three did not transmit at all and Kestrel read names to the hiss like setting out food for a cat that might be dead.
But at the end of it the voice came back with two names and four words apiece, spoken carefully, like carrying full cups: "Both on a yard roll.
Alive.
Working.
Together."
*Together.*
Kestrel put her forehead on the cold key and did not transmit for a while.
"My name is Sable," the voice said then, unasked — the last true thing, given free, which is how you know what a thing is worth.
"If you use it on this band, they will post me somewhere without windows.
Say it wrong on purpose."
"Thank you, Signal Watch Three," Kestrel said, and heard, four light-minutes away, aboard the machine that had eaten her family, an enemy laugh for the first time.
It was Rook, of course, who caught her — cheats know stairs — sitting in the dark of the loft one eighth-night with the set warm and the whip aimed at the corridor.

He listened long enough to understand it, which took him one hand of listening, then dealt himself onto the spare crate and said nothing to anyone, ever, which in the dorm's economy was a fortune spent quietly on her behalf.
"You know what you're betting," he said at last.
It wasn't a question.
"A friend across a war, Kes.
Biggest bet in the book."
"I know the rule."
"Can you laugh it off?"
She thought about *together*, and about the fourth name and the fortieth, and about a hymn that was mostly multiplication.
"No," she said.

"Then don't lose," Rook said, and cut the deck, and dealt two hands under a sealed transmitter mast — one for her, one for the hiss — while the open band carried its two small voices across an interdiction line that all the grown wars had built, and nobody who owned the band was listening yet.
In the margin of her school slate, where auditors never look because margins have no yield, Kestrel kept her own ledger now, the family habit.
Three entries: *Sable, who checked.
Together.*
And beneath them, pressed so hard the stylus tore the wax, a name she did not write to remember.
She wrote it the way her cousin wrote it.
So she would never need to.

Commander Draco came home to Cydon for the harvest the way he had come home for eighteen harvests of a line officer's life: on the last boat that could still be called on time, in a uniform he took off at the gate, into work that did not care what rank had been taken off with it.
The terraces were in full cut.
The whole valley smelled of bruised greens and turned water, and from the high sluice the harvesters walked the contour lines like patient grey beetles, his son Petyr driving the big one.
Petyr drove the way Maren cooked — without waste, without commentary — and thanked every star in the catalogue that he could not fly.
On Cydon that almost counted as a trade.

Draco spent his first day home lying in the mud of the number-two sluice gate with his arm in the works, and Maren stood over him handing down tools and their twenty years of marriage in the usual dialect: *the left seal.
No, the left.
You've gone soft, Commander.*
An officer's hands remember farm work the way the mouth remembers a first language.
The skiff came over on the second day.

It came high and silent out of the sunward haze, wearing no registry the valley's old scopes could resolve — a Synod survey skiff, flying what the Synod's diplomats, who still existed that year, called a mapping flight.
It did not threaten anything.
It photographed everything.
It flew the terraces in long patient rows, the way the harvesters walked them, and Draco stood in the sluice mud and felt a cold that had no rank insignia and needed none.
*They were counting.*

He had read the bulletins like every line officer: the tithe hardening in the outer chain, an interdiction line posted off the Halcyon approaches, ore boats running it because starving slower was not a plan.
War was still a rumor out there.
But a rumor does not fly survey rows over your wife's terraces, patient, indifferent, and larger than anything you could do about it.
The Coalition air-watch posted a valley alert before the skiff had finished its third row: all civil drones grounded until further notice.
Which mattered, because of Sorrel.

Sorrel was the neighbors' child, from the next terrace east — nine years old, mud to the elbows, a gap where a tooth was in no hurry — and she flew the family's seeder drone the way weeds grow: low, fast, everywhere, and with a total absence of official permission.

Draco had watched her run the terrace lips all week, dropping the drone below the stone shoulders and snapping it out over the water gates, and had said nothing, because it was the most natural flying he had seen in years, and some instruction you can only ruin.
She had the seeder up when the alert came down.
Of course she did.
She was nine, and alerts travel at the speed of adults.
The militia flyer that came to log the violation was polite about it, which made it worse.
Unlicensed flight during a survey alert: the drone impounded, the family fined, and — this was the part that put the cold back in Draco's spine — the pilot entered on the incident roll.
A name, an age, and the notation *air-capable*, going into a file, and Draco had spent eighteen years learning where files go.
Lists outlive the men who write them.
In the outer chain, lists like that one had already been converted into levies.
If the Synod ever came for Cydon's tithe, it would not ask first for ore.
It would ask for the files.
"Log the flight as mine," Draco said, in the neighbors' gate, in the voice that had moved squadrons.
The militia sergeant looked up.
"Commander Draco, third lane squadron, on harvest leave.
I had the machine up checking control trim.
Instrument proficiency.
The child was observing."

The sergeant knew it for a lie — the whole east terrace had watched that drone dance the water gates all week — and wrote it down anyway, because a commander's name is a coin, and Draco was choosing, visibly, in front of witnesses, to spend it.
The drone came back minus its transmitter.
The incident roll carried one name that day, and it was not nine years old.
Sorrel appeared at his elbow while he signed.
She had watched the whole transaction with the black-eyed attention of a child who has just seen the adult world through a gap in it.
"Why?" she said.
"Because you fly better than any list you'd be put on," Draco said.
He handed her the gutted drone; she took it like a wounded bird.
"Stay off lists, girl.
All of them, as long as you can.
They are the only thing in the sky that never misses."
"I'll owe you," she said — a farm child; the ledger reflex arrives before the second teeth.
"You'll pay it to somebody else, years from now, and not know you're doing it," he said.
"That's how terraces work.
Water comes in at the top."
That night Maren put supper down and sat across from him and did the thing she did instead of shouting, which was to be precise.
"You spent your name today on the neighbors' drone."
"It's my name."
"You'll spend it one day where it doesn't buy a citation," she said.
"You'll spend it where it costs the whole account, and you'll do it the same way you did it today.
Without asking me."
It was not an accusation but a weather report, from the person who had studied his sky longest.
He had no answer that was not a lie, so he passed her the bread.

The evening band was reading the lane bulletins to the valley: the interdiction line off Halcyon, a staff directive out of Ranger Command about *consolidated defensive lines* and *positions of unfavorable retention* — staff language, Draco translated for the table, for places we mean to leave.
Petyr jerked his chin at the pale coin rising over the east ridge.
"Half the east terraces have cousins taking homestead grants up there now.
Eight hundred berths in the survey, they're saying.
Is the moon a position of unfavorable retention?"

Everyone looked at it: Cydon's moon, which had a catalogue number that year and no name anyone used, low and mild over the harvest, its first-wave habitat lights just visible on the terminator like sparks caught in wool.
"It's high ground with no way down," Draco said slowly, an officer answering, and then, because Maren was watching him, the farmer finished it: "And it'll have people on it by spring.
So the staff can draw what it likes.
The order is wrong when the people are still on the ground."
Maren looked at him the way you look at a man quoting the epitaph he has not ordered yet, and said nothing, and put the kettle on.
The races ran on his last night — the rings lit up across the valley like a necklace flung over the hills, and the whole east terrace went up to the fence lines to watch.
Sorrel stood on the middle rail between Draco and Petyr, calling the turns half a breath before the commentator.
That one, number seven, with the scorched stripe on her tail — the champion, the one who brakes later than anybody breathing.
"I counted," Sorrel said, eyes full of pylon light.
"Nobody's later.
One day I'll be later."
"You'd race?"
Draco asked.

"I'd win," said the child the militia had nearly filed, and the number seven came off the last ring so low over the terraces that the crowd's gasp and the engine arrived together, and Draco, watching Sorrel watch Vela, noted in the ledger he kept behind his eyes that this valley was full of things worth more than any line the staff would ever consolidate.
His recall came two days early, as he had known in his spine it would: line officers to report, leave curtailed, something off Halcyon that the bulletin declined to finish describing.
Maren folded his collar down at the gate and did not say be careful, because in twenty years she never had; she said, "Come back for the planting," which was harder and better.
The boat lifted at dawn.

He watched Cydon drop away with the harvest half in, the terraces printed gold and green on the world like an audit nobody feared yet, and the moon came around the limb with its little sparks of homestead light, catalogued, unconsolidated, undefended.
It was, that year, only a moon.
The name it would carry was still years off, waiting for him the way spent coins wait, and this book will get there; everything in the lanes gets there.
Water comes in at the top.
The wounded came into Vantor on the third night of winter, and the war stopped being a rumor at 0140, ward time, which is the only time this chapter keeps.

Afterward the lane bulletins would call it the first skirmish, as if it had been on a schedule: ore boats out of Ferrus running the interdiction line off the Halcyon approaches, a Synod corvette screen that did not hail before it fired, a Coalition lane patrol that answered, eleven minutes of everyone being surprised, and then two fleets discovering they had no plan at all for the part after the shooting — the part that is only burned people, and distance.

Mira was twenty-two, a field-medic with two solo flights in her logbook, and she was aboard her cousin Selene's salvage tug when the distress went out — the season's arrangement: Selene flew the graveyard watch, Mira rode the belt as medic.

Her brother Corin had died in the spring — a treatable fever, four hours from medicine that sat forty hours at a Synod checkpoint being weighed — and Mira had begun flying lessons the week after the funeral, on a theory she could not yet say aloud: the war would not slow down to clinic speed, so the clinic would have to learn lane speed.
The ore boat had taken the corvettes' first burst along her engineering spaces, and her aft hatch had jammed one-third open with a fire behind it and a stoker inside it.
The cut-team wanted to wait for the spreader bar.
The fire was not waiting for the spreader bar.

So Mira put her left hand into the gap and held the hatch's hot edge off its track — nine seconds, she counted them, she would count them for the rest of her life on bad nights — while Selene took the stoker under the arms and hauled her through into the world of the living.
She did not feel the palm until the glove came off with skin in it.
The scar it left ran corner to corner, a laser-neat diagonal she would carry into every cockpit she ever flew — the cheapest thing anyone paid that night.
The stoker's name was Ember, which the ward found funny in the way wards find things funny — quietly, so the dying don't hear.
Nineteen years old, off the Ferrus boats, burned along her right side from ankle to jaw, and alive, because a hatch had stayed open nine seconds longer than physics had voted for.

Vantor's clinic quarter took the overflow; her father's free clinic was a burn ward by 0200, cots in the vaccination line, Doctor Ansel moving down the rows doing the arithmetic the Synod had made of medicine: dermal packs counted like ammunition, because they had been tithed like it; half the quarter's stock had spent the autumn at checkpoints being weighed.

Her father rationed mercy in grams that night, and Mira understood that this too was Kaine's doctrine — the Hegemon does not board a world; it closes the lane and lets the clinics do its conquering — and that it was working.
Ember woke on the third day, inside the mask, and her first words through the burn gauze were: "You're the hatch."
"I'm the medic," Mira said.
"Vantor girl."
The voice was a rasp run through a grate.
"Everything on Vantor gets weighed.
I've hauled your manifests.
So weigh it.
What do I owe?"
"Nothing.
Heal."
"Wrong scales."
Ember's unburned eye came around to her, stubborn as a bolt seized in its thread.
"The corvette didn't hail.
It weighed us and it fired, and my boat's four best are ash on the lane, and I am lying here on somebody's nine seconds.
What.
Do I owe."
Mira sat down on the cot's edge; her legs were done pretending.
"I keep a book," she said.
"Since spring."
She did not say Corin's name; she never said names before they were stable, and the dead never are.
"Everyone counts the killed — the bulletins, the Synod, both fleets.
Let them have that column.
I keep the other one.
You're in it.
Entry one.
That's your whole debt: you stay in my column."
The eye held her a long time.
Burn wards teach you to read a face from one-third of it.
"Then I keep a book too," Ember said finally.
"Entry one: the hatch."
Her voice went down to grate and iron.
"And a second column, for the scales that fired on a boat full of stokers.
Tell me his name — the one whose scales those are.
The one who closed the lane."
"Kaine," Mira said.
"Kaine," Ember repeated, once, like an entry, and slept.
Selene came at 0300 between salvage sorties, still in her deck harness, carrying dock-food and forty waking hours as lightly as she carried everything else.
She was kin — the family had split, two generations back, into medicine and salvage, and called both rescue.
She read Ember's chart, then the ward, unblinking, the way she had been since they were children under a shared summer roof: Selene did not look at things; she stood watch over them.
At bed nine she stopped.
Bed nine was the ward's quiet argument: a Synod gunner, pulled by Selene's own grapple from a corvette section the rest of his picket had not come back for.
They do not come back; retreat is theft.
The gunner had recited the doctrine to the ward himself, in pieces, through burned lips, as if the rule that had orphaned him in the sky were a kind of painkiller.
"He'd have weighed you," Selene said.
Not cruelly; it was the argument the whole Coalition was about to spend thirty years having, and she opened it the way kin do — without ceremony, at 0300.
"A wound doesn't have a side," Mira said.
"It has a uniform."

"Then it's lucky," Mira said, "that I treat the wound and burn the uniform," and Selene looked at her cousin for a moment, and something that was almost a smile crossed all that vigilance, and she pulled the ward's one good chair to where every cot was in her sightline, and said, "Sleep three hours.
I have the watch."
She said it the way other people say amen.
Unsleeping, Mira understood much later, was not a temperament.
It was a doctrine being drafted that winter in a tug and a burn ward: stand the watch ahead of the scream, not behind it.
What Selene would one day found was already sitting in that chair at 0300.
This book notes the timestamp.
The gunner in bed nine asked Mira, on the fifth night, for the only medicine she ever refused to give.
"Don't log me rescued," he whispered.
"If I'm alive, I retreated.
The rolls reach my family.
Log me dead."
She would not falsify a chart; that was her father in her.
But she sat a long time with the admissions slate, then entered him as *unidentified, non-ambulatory* — true, since she had never said his name, and within her own superstition he had none yet.
If the Synod's rolls could weaponize a man's survival against his kin, her ward's records owed those rolls nothing, not even arithmetic.
It was the first document of the war she ever bent — toward a family she would never meet, on the enemy's side of the line.
The habit was contagious that year.
It had started with an assessor.
Ironwood sat in the corridor all that week: Ember's cousin, off the ore boats, a big quiet man who had carried one end of every stretcher on the third night and then simply not left.
He did not ask twice.
Each dawn when Mira came off shift he stood, said "Today?", and each dawn she said "Not yet," and he sat back down and resumed holding the steel mess-cup he never drank from.

On the morning she finally said "Today — she's asking for you," he set the cup on the bench, and Mira saw that over the week it had quietly acquired the shape of his grip, folded like paper at the rim.
Slow anger is still anger.
It just keeps better books.
That night, by the stock-lamp, Mira ruled a fresh page into two columns.
Over the right-hand column she wrote SAVED and entered: a stoker, nineteen, of Ferrus.
A gunner, unnamed, of the fleet that would not come back for him.
Eleven more, by trade and world, no names until stable.
The left-hand column she left headed with nothing, and empty — the bulletins could count that side; the Synod could; both fleets kept that ledger in triplicate.
Somewhere up the lane a man was teaching the whole sky his arithmetic, one closed lane, one weighed convoy, one burned boat at a time.
She had started the counter-book.
Nineteen days after the burning, Halcyon Station still held its shape, the way ash holds the shape of the log.
You could walk the dock ring and name everything by what it had been: the manifest office, the number-four fabricator's empty socket, the bread stall, the other bread stall.
Hale walked it daily with chalk and slate, cataloguing his station into the past tense; his limp had stopped being a limp and become the pace of the place.
The tithe had come for the things; then for the people; in the end for the place itself, and what it could not take it had burned — the Synod's doctrine reduced to its final arithmetic.
Hale had put the tithe-dorm children on the grain boats two weeks before, on nothing but his spine's advice; it was the last order he gave as stationmaster that was obeyed by everyone it covered.
He kept the thought of those boats the way other men keep brandy.
On the nineteenth day his crews dug the dock bell out of the slag under the ring chapel.
The old rolls said it was cast in the same pour as a Warden cockpit bell, when the station was a consecrated waypoint of the old Order.
The burning had run it like a candle: a third of the lip gone to a frozen spill, the crown cracked through.
It would not ring as it was.
Hale, who had sworn the old covenant at nineteen and been ground-side with a shattered hip when the Order flew to the Sundering, could not order it melted for salvage.
He had it set upright on Dock Nine.
A bell that cannot ring is still a bell — just a bell in the past tense, and it matched the station.
Its sister, men said, still flew, bolted in an old Warden cockpit, kept by the kind of pilot who names the dead at dusk.
The relief flotilla made orbit that afternoon, and Vale came down with the first boat.
Hale had known him twenty years, since Vale wintered at Halcyon as a convoy lieutenant with a bad haircut, the quarantine season the station fed stranded crews out of its margin.
You know a man by what he does in a margin.
Vale crossed the ruined dock ring in a warden's plain coat and said none of the things relief officers say.
He stood a while at the bell, then said, "Show me," and Hale showed him: the ring, the sockets, the past tense.
Vale listened as if testimony were cargo and he was signing for every crate.
He had brought the future down with him, in triplicate.

The first of it was his adjutant, Aurelia — young, ink-stained at the second knuckle, carrying a field recorder and a bound paper archive-book, because, she said, recordings fail and paper burns, so you keep each to shame the other.
She wrote down everything, including what the ruin smelled like.
Hale trusted her at once; nobody lies about smells.
The second was a judge of the league's tribunal service, immaculate to the boot-tops in a world of ash, and his name was Vargo.
He had come, he said, to apportion responsibility.
He said it the way a lens clicks.
He required the manifests of every boat that had run the interdiction: their defiance had furnished the Synod its pretext, and the league's case must establish the chain of provocation.
Events, he said, have authors; a tribunal's work is attribution.
"The manifests burned with the manifest office," Hale said.
"If you want the author of a burned station, start with the man who ordered the burning.
You're the attribution man — bill whoever's cheapest."
He had buried eleven hundred people's home in nineteen days of past tense; he watched the judge decide, with judicial economy, to hate him.
"You would bill the drowned for the water," Hale said, making it worse, because some purchases are worth it.
"I keep excellent records, stationmaster," Vargo said, gently, and wrote something small and permanent in his folio.
"Yours is begun."
Aurelia's recorder caught the sound — a scrape, in the gutted cargo bays behind Dock Nine, where the search teams had twice called it clear.

Vale put up one hand for silence and went in alone, and Hale watched from the bay mouth as he did the thing that made the rest of this book happen: took off his sidearm and laid it on the deck in plain view, sat on his heels in somebody's wrecked cargo netting, and waited eleven minutes without a word.
Out from behind the fire-fused pallet stack came a boy of about six, grey as the deck, holding a brass bread-stall token the way drowning men hold rope.
He had been drinking condensation off a coolant line.
He did not cry.
He watched Vale with the flat attention of the very small who have decided the universe requires auditing, then crossed the deck and put his free hand into the Warden's, and that was all.
Vale carried him out into the light.

A Vantor medic took him at the triage line, and it is recorded — Aurelia recorded it — that the boy would not surrender the token, so she logged him in as *bearer, one loaf, outstanding*, and nobody who wrote that was entirely joking.
"Note that sentiment is not policy," Vargo said, into the stiff quiet after.
"An officer cannot simply—" "Write this," Vale said to Aurelia.
He had not raised his voice; he had only stopped being interruptible.
He put his hand on the dead bell, and she took it down as he said it, and the paper copy survives, and this is the whole of it: "I, Vale, Warden, at Halcyon Station, in sight of what was done here.
The tithe came for the things, then for the people, then for the place.
So, by the old covenant — every free system a hearth, every Warden a door — I swear: the lane past Halcyon stays open.
While one Warden is breathing, the lane stays open.
What they close, we hold open; what they burn, we build ahead of them; and any hand raised in surrender finds a door, not a gun, because we will not learn their arithmetic.
I did not ask the league's leave and I do not offer the league my apology.
Write that I swore it without authority.
Write that I swore it anyway.
And when they ask under what law: under the bell."
"The compact," Vargo said into the silence, "gives no officer the power to swear the league into a war" — and he was correct; this book will be fair to him: correct, and it saved no one.
He would spend his life keeping excellent records of men who had been necessary instead of correct, in a folio that grew teeth.
"The war was sworn the day they weighed the first crew," Vale said.
"I'm signing the receipt."

Vargo's launch was gone by dusk, carrying its dissent into the future — and Hale, who had watched the judge's face during the oath, had seen what a man looks like when history happens without his signature on it.
At dusk Aurelia set the archive-book on the bell's cracked crown for a desk and asked Hale for his account: name and office.
"Hale," he said.
"Stationmaster of what you're standing in."
She wrote it without pity, then read the oath back once in her clerk's steady voice, and Hale listened to it go into the permanent tense.
An oath, he had believed at nineteen, was a shield; at fifty, a debt the dead leave the living.
He was sixty-one now, his station was a bell that could not ring, and a boy was asleep in the triage line refusing to surrender a token worth one loaf, outstanding.
"Add my name," Hale said.
"Small letters.
I've done this before, and I know what it costs."
Aurelia looked up.
"For the record — you're the first after him."
"God help the record," Hale said.
She asked him, last, recorder running, the question her archive kept a blank page for: "Stationmaster.
For the archive.
What is an oath?"

Hale put his palm flat on the ruined bell — which would ring again or would not, depending on everything this book has yet to tell — and gave the archive its answer: "It's the number you write when the arithmetic fails, and the remainder is people."

Above them, bay by bay, the relief lamps were coming on across the ruin — not many, and not bright, and absolutely certain of the shape they meant to fill — and out past the dock ring the first re-flagged convoy cleared the roads, outbound down the lane, which was, that night and every night after that anyone could make it so, open.
